coredata.dc:description: Treatment in combination with real-time monitoring of dynamic organelle changes is of great significance for evaluating therapeutic efficacy. However, it remains highly challenging to realize such specific treatment and monitoring in organelles. Herein, we developed one kind of red emissive two-photon carbon dots (TP-CDs) via an N doping strategy for nucleolus-targeted treatment combined with real-time monitoring of dynamic changes in the nucleolus. In the TP-CD system, N doping endows TP-CDs with intrinsic two-photon excitation, red emission at 605 nm, and singlet oxygen (1O2) production under 638 nm laser irradiation. More importantly, N heterocycles induced by doping offers significant affinity for TP-CDs toward RNA to realize specific self-targeting toward nucleolus and fluorescence variation during the photodynamic therapy (PDT) via the cleaving of RNA chain by 1O2 to dissociate TP-CDs@RNA complex. By combining 1O2 production, fluorescent variation during the PDT process, long-wavelength excitation and emission characteristics, as well as specific self-targetability of nucleolus, TP-CDs can be considered as a kind of “smart” CDs to realize treatment in combination of real-time dynamic changes in nucleolus for the first time.

Absorption spectrum (a), TP action cross-section (b), one-photon excitation and emission spectra (red line: excitation, blue line: emission) (c), and two-photon emission spectrum excited by a 700 nm two-photon laser (d) of TP-CDs. Inset in (a): Photographs of the TP-CD solution under dark (left) and 365 nm UV irradiation (right). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

1. (c) Fluorescence spectra of TP-CDs and RNA with the addition of RNAse from 0 to 30 min. (d) 1H NMR spectra of TP-CDs in the absence and presence of RNA. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(c) Fluorescence (left), bright-field (middle), and merged (right) images of HeLa cells after coincubation with TP-CDs. Control group: no TP-CDs, laser irradiation. TP-CD group: TP-CDs at 50 μg mL−1, no laser irradiation. TP-CDs + Laser group: TP-CDs at 50 μg mL−1, laser irradiation. Green fluorescence represents high DCF levels, indicating high 1O2 levels that oxidized DCFH (488 nm excitation). Fluorescence intensities were proportional to the 1O2 concentration. Scale bar: 100 μm. Irradiation was performed under a 638 nm laser at a power of 1.0 W cm−2, and irradiation time was 5 min in (c). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The cells were cultured with TP-CDs (50 μg mL−1). Scale bar: 100 μm. (d) Fluorescence spectra of TP-CDs and RNA solution under 638 nm laser irradiation (1.0 W/cm−2) for different times. (e) RNA electrophoresis after different pretreatments (a: no pretreatment; b: pretreatment with TP-CDs and irradiation, b: pretreatment with RNAse). Irradiation was performed using 638 nm laser (1.0 W cm−2 ) for 5 min. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a–d) CLSM images of HeLa cells treated with TP-CDs in the presence of 638 nm laser irradiation (1.0 W cm−2) for different times (a–d: 0, 1, 3, 5 min). Scale bar: 20 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Red emissive two-photon carbon dots: Photodynamic therapy in combination with real-time dynamic monitoring for the nucleolus
head.ce:dedication: Dedicated to the 100th anniversary of chemistry in Nankai University.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Treatment in combination with real-time monitoring of dynamic organelle changes is of great significance for evaluating therapeutic efficacy. However, it remains highly challenging to realize such specific treatment and monitoring in organelles. Herein, we developed one kind of red emissive two-photon carbon dots (TP-CDs) via an N doping strategy for nucleolus-targeted treatment combined with real-time monitoring of dynamic changes in the nucleolus. In the TP-CD system, N doping endows TP-CDs with intrinsic two-photon excitation, red emission at 605 nm, and singlet oxygen (1O2) production under 638 nm laser irradiation. More importantly, N heterocycles induced by doping offers significant affinity for TP-CDs toward RNA to realize specific self-targeting toward nucleolus and fluorescence variation during the photodynamic therapy (PDT) via the cleaving of RNA chain by 1O2 to dissociate TP-CDs@RNA complex. By combining 1O2 production, fluorescent variation during the PDT process, long-wavelength excitation and emission characteristics, as well as specific self-targetability of nucleolus, TP-CDs can be considered as a kind of “smart” CDs to realize treatment in combination of real-time dynamic changes in nucleolus for the first time.

ce:section.ce:para: Treatment combined with real-time monitoring of intracellular dynamic changes, especially for specific organelles, is of great significance to evaluate therapeutic efficacy [1–4]. Among various organelles, the nucleolus is the ribosome factory for the synthesis and assembly of ribosomal subunits, which is strongly related to the response of tumor-suppressing genes. A disruption of nucleolar function can induce a series of errors in ribosomal biogenesis to interfere with the growth and proliferation of tumor cells [5–7]. As a result, the nucleolus has been considered an ideal target for various cancer therapies [8–12]. However, it remains a highly challenging task for therapeutic agents to realize treatment in combination with real-time monitoring of dynamic changes in the nucleolus because of the lack of appropriate therapeutic agents together with nucleolus-targeted features and variations in imaging signals during the therapeutic process [13,14]. The lack of nucleolus-targeted specificity induces nonspecific interactions with all cellular components in complex biological systems, significantly affecting accuracy and efficacy [15–17]. In particular, such nonspecificity has a significant impact on the therapeutic efficacy of photodynamic therapy (PDT) due to the instantaneous lifetime (less than 40 ns) and diffusion distance (≈20 nm) of reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced in PDT [18,19]. To guarantee the effectiveness of their oxidation potentials, photosensitizers used in PDT should be located close to the target RNA via nucleolus targeting. Moreover, the realization of the real-time monitoring of dynamic changes during the therapeutic process significantly depends on the imaging signal variation accompanied by treatment [20–22]. However, most of the already reported therapeutic agents have no imaging function [23–25]. Even if they have imaging functions, such imaging signals are usually insusceptible to surrounding environmental changes caused by the treatment, resulting in the lack of essential response signals [26–30]. Therefore, the development of multifunctional therapeutic agents that integrate nucleolus-targeted features and variations in imaging signals during the therapeutic process is critical to realize treatment in combination with real-time monitoring of dynamic changes in the nucleolus.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of metal-free and highly fluorescent zero-dimensional nanomaterials [31–33]. They have received increasing attention in various biomedical applications (e.g., bioimaging, drug delivery, phototherapy) because of their outstanding intrinsic advantages [34–37].These advantages also enable them to have promising potential applications in the field of treatment combined with real-time monitoring, particularly when considering that CDs may possess phototherapeutic functions. Despite of these substantial advantages and that several examples of CDs have be reported to apply for nucleolus-targeted imaging or PDT have been reported [12,38,39], there is still no report on the application of CDs in the field of treatment combining with real-time monitoring for nucleolus, owing to lack of therapeutic CDs integrating with nucleolus-targeted feature and variation of fluorescence signal during the therapeutic process. Besides, CDs still suffer from an inherent shortcoming to restrict the application of fluorescence signal for real-time monitoring, namely, short-wavelength excitation and emission [40,41]. Shortwave excitation and emission can cause massive interference from the background absorption and emission of biological tissues, significantly reducing the tissue penetration depths of CDs' light-mediated theragnostics [33,42]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop therapeutic CDs with longwave excitation and emission for further treatment combined with real-time monitoring.

Based on this understanding, we herein attempt to develop one kind of red emissive two-photon CDs (TP-CDs) for treatment in combination with real-time monitoring of dynamic changes in the nucleolus. Specifically, p-aminoazobenzene (PAB) was used as a carbon resource to hydrothermally synthesize TP-CDs via an N doping strategy (Scheme 1 ). As already reported for heteroatom doping CDs [43–45], such N doping can significantly affect the emission and excitation properties of CDs, endowing TP-CDs with intrinsic two-photon excitation characteristics and red emissive features at a wavelength of 605 nm. In addition to longwave excitation and emission, ROS production can also be achieved under 638 nm laser irradiation, providing an enormous possibility for PDT. More importantly, N heterocycles induced by doping have been confirmed using various characterizations and theoretical simulation to play a key role in TP-CDs' affinity toward RNA, similar to already reported dyes with N heterocyclic structures [46–48]. Such affinity not only offers possibility to realize specific self-targeting toward nucleolus, but also endows fluorescent variation during the therapeutic process via the cleaving of RNA chain by ROS to dissociate TP-CDs@RNA complex. By combining ROS production capability, fluorescence variation during the PDT process, long-wavelength excitation and emission characteristics, as well as specific self-targetability of nucleolus, TP-CDs can be considered as a kind of “smart” CDs to realize treatment in combination of real-time of dynamic changes in nucleolus for the first time.

p-Aminoazobenzene (PAB), 1,3-diphenylbenzofuran (DPBF), 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperide (TEMP) 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) and rhodamine 6G were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. Ethanol alcohol, methanol alcohol and dichloromethane were purchased from Shanghai General-reagent Co. Ltd. Silica gel (mesh number: 200–300) was purchased from Qingdao Haiyang Chemical Co. Ltd. RNA, RNA, DNAse and RNAse were purchased from Beijing Solarbio Life Science Co. Ltd. 2′,7′-Dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) and 4’,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole, dihydrochloride (DAPI) were purchased from Shanghai Beyotime Life Science Co. Ltd. SYTO™ RNASelect™ Green Fluorescent Cell Stain (RNA Select) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd. NaN3, colchicine (CLC), chlorpromazine (CPZ), and methyl-β-cyclodextrin (MβCD) were purchased from Dacheng Chemicals Co., Ltd.. Calcein-AM and propidium iodide were purchased from Shanghai Yeasen Life Science Co., Ltd. Roswell Park Memorial Institute 1640 and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Gibco BRL Co. Ltd. All reagents were used without further purification.

The TP-CDs were prepared by the solvothermal synthesis method. First, PAB (0.15 g) was dissolved in ethanol alcohol (15 mL), and then the solution was diverted to a polytetrafluoroethylene equipped steel autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 10 h. The reactor was cooled to room temperature, and then a mixed solution of CDs was obtained. The solution was purified by silica column chromatography using mixed ethanol and dichloromethane as the eluent. Finally, the CDs we acquired with red fluorescence were dispersed in ethanol alcohol.

A JFM-2100 transmission electron microscope operating at 200 kV was used to obtain high-resolution transmission electron microscopy images. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analyses were performed using a Rigaku X-ray Powder Diffractometer. The mass spectra were measured by Bruker SMART Mass spectrometry. The absorption spectra were measured on a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrometer. The fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer. The FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Nicolet Nexus 470 FTIR spectrometer. The Raman spectra were recorded using a Renishaw Raman spectrometer at an excitation wavelength of 546 nm. Zeta potentials were measured using a ZEN3600 dynamic laser light scattering instrument. 1H and 13C NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker Avance III spectrometer. The ESR spectra were measured by Bruke EMXmicro electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometry. The XPS spectra were measured by Thermo Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometry. Confocal microscopy imaging was measured by a Leica TCS SP8 DIVE confocal laser scanning microscope.

TP-CDs were synthesized by employing PAB as a precursor in ethanol solution via a one-step solvothermal method (Scheme 1). The morphology of the as-synthesized TP-CDs was primarily studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a, TP-CDs displayed monodispersed quasi-spherical morphology, illustrating that they consisted of nanodots. Their average sizes were determined to be 4.0 ± 1.1 nm, with uniform size distribution, and the narrow size distribution indicated that TP-CDs were uniform in size (Fig. S1). High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images showed that TP-CDs had a crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm (inset of Fig. 1a), assigned to the (100) plane of the graphitic carbon core [49–51]. The crystalline structure of the graphitic carbon core was further supported by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis. It showed a distinct diffraction peak at 2θ = 23° (Fig. S2), attributed to the (002) plane of the graphene structure. In addition, the Raman spectrum of TP-CDs was recorded to probe the hybridization state of the carbon. As shown in Fig. S3, TP-CDs showed two Raman peaks at approximately 1392 cm−1 (D band) of sp3 hybridization and 1524 cm−1 (G band) of sp2 hybridization (Fig. S3). It is a widely used method to determine the rate of graphite, that is, the structural order between crystalline and amorphous carbon defined by the ratio of ID/IG in CDs [52,53]. Herein, the ID/IG of the TP-CDs was 0.66. This indicated a relatively higher content of sp2 graphitic carbon atoms than that of disordered sp3 carbon atoms in the TP-CDs. Besides, a high overlapping between the D and G bands was observed. Referring to previous studies on CDs [54–56], such unusual profile with overlapping may be due to the relatively low carbonization temperature. In this work, the reaction temperature (180 °C) is lower than those (200–300 °C) previously used [55]. Moreover, structural details were further revealed by 13C NMR measurements (Fig. 1b). It is known that sp3 and sp2 carbons have different chemical environments, so 13C NMR signals are different, the former in the range of 8–80 ppm but the latter in the range of 90–180 ppm. As shown in Fig. 1b, the 13C NMR spectrum of the TP-CDs in D2O displayed four main distinct chemical shift peaks at 16.7, 57.4, 116.7, and 127.9 ppm. The peak at 127.9 ppm was due to aromatic sp2 carbons, and the peak at 116.7 ppm was attributed to terminal sp2 carbons, while the peaks at 16.7 and 57.4 ppm were assigned to saturated sp3 carbons. Combining the results obtained from TEM, XRD, Raman, and 13C NMR, we could infer the successful synthesis of TP-CDs that were mainly composed of sp2 graphitic carbon atoms.

To gain further insight into the components and structure of TP-CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), mass spectrometry (MS), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) were performed. In the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S4), three peaks at 285.1, 399.1 and 532.1 eV were observed, attributable to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The C1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into several components (Fig. 1c). Specifically, the components with 284.8 eV binding energies were attributed to graphitic carbon atoms. The other components were assigned to C–N (285.6 eV). In the N1s spectrum (Fig. 1d), the peaks at 398.6 and 399.8 eV could be assigned to pyridine nitrogen, and quaternary nitrogen, respectively. In addition, XPS data displayed that amounts of C, N, and O in the TP-CD surface were 87.50%, 6.39%, and 6.10%, respectively. Moreover, MS measurements more clearly revealed the structural features of TP-CDs. Specifically, as shown in Fig. 1e, two distinct MS peaks were found at 309.1 and 331.5, possibly assigned to two kinds of quinoline derivatives (inset of Fig. 1e). Furthermore, FT-IR was also performed to detect various functional groups in the TP-CDs (Fig. 1f). The FT-IR spectrum of TP-CDs exhibited a broad characteristic absorption peak centered at 3344 cm−1, resulting from the stretching vibration of the N–H/O–H group. The peaks located at 1231, 1508, and 1627 cm−1 correspond to C–N bending vibration, N–H bending vibration, and CC stretching vibration, respectively. The peaks at 813 and 3186 cm−1 were due to Ar–H bending and stretching vibrations, respectively. The peaks at 2850 and 2920 cm−1 were assigned to characteristic CH2 stretching vibrations. In addition, the peaks at 3344, 2920, and 2850 cm−1 distinctly differing from those of PAB implied that the TP-CDs had more N–H/O–H and CH2 groups than the carbonization precursor. These XPS, MS, and FT-IR results indicated the presence of –OH, –NH2, and N heterocyclic structures in the TP-CDs.

After detailed characterization of TP-CDs, we further surveyed the properties of excitation and emission. In the absorption spectrum (Fig. 2 a), TP-CDs displayed a sharp absorption peak at 274 nm and a broad absorption band from 400 to 600 nm. Referring to previous studies on CDs [57–59], the former was ascribed to the N heterocyclic structure on the surface or edge of the TP-CDs, and the latter was due to the aromatic π system of TP-CDs. In the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 2c), TP-CDs had a strong one-photon fluorescence emission peak at 605 nm when excited at 546 nm, corresponding to red fluorescence. The remarkable red fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation further demonstrated the red-emitting feature of TP-CDs (Fig. 3 a, inset). As control, one type of N-free CDs was synthesized [60], displaying absorption peak at 264 nm and fluorescence emission peak at 342 nm (Fig. S5a). Both absorption and emission wavelengths of N-free CDs were much shorter than those of TP-CDs, implying that N doping have significant effect on the absorption and emission properties of CDs. More importantly, TP-CDs were shown to possess two-photon absorption characteristics. As shown in Fig. 2b, the two-photon absorption of TP-CDs ranged from 680 to 1000 nm. After excitation, the emission peak was located at approximately 600 nm (Fig. 2d), and it was identical to the homologous one-photon excited fluorescence emission. The quantum yields of TP-CDs were 15.1% for one-photon excitation (excited at 546 nm) and 18.2% for two-photon excitation (excited at 700 nm). This two-photon excitation characteristic of TP-CDs could be attributed to the multiphoton absorption process, similar to previous reports [34,61]. In addition, the two-photon absorption cross-section σ(λ) of TP-CDs was calculated by obtaining the specimen and reference fluorescence intensities, according to previous studies [62]. Rhodamine 6G was used as the reference, and the average σ(λ) of TP-CDs at an excitation of 700 nm was calculated to be 1450 ± 60 GM (Goeppert-Mayer unit, with 1 GM = 10 −50 cm4 s/photon). This value was comparable with those of other previously reported two-photon CDs [63–65]. This two-photon excitation characteristic of TP-CDs provides potential possibilities for the bioimaging of living cells and deep tissues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The affinity of TP-CDs toward RNA
ce:section.ce:para:
Dyes with N heterocyclic structures, especially quinoline structures, have been reported to have an affinity toward RNA, i.e., (E)-2-(2-(1H-indol-3-yl)vinyl)quinaldine, (E)-2-(2,4,6-trimethoxystyryl)quinaldine, (E)-4-(2-(6-methoxy quinaldine-2-yl)vinyl)-N,N-dimethylaniline (Table S1). The abovementioned XPS, MS, and FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1d, e, and 1f) proved the existence of an N heterocyclic structure in TP-CDs. Encouraged by these factors, the interaction between TP-CDs, RNA, and other substances, including DNA and protein, was studied using fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 3a, fluorescence enhancement was observed when RNA was added to the TP-CD solution. In contrast, insignificant fluorescence enhancement was found for TP-CD solutions with other substances, including DNA and protein. With the gradual addition of RNA into TP-CD aqueous solution, we observed fluorescence enhancement. Subsequently, RNAse was added to this solution, and the fluorescence decrease was observed with increasing time (Fig. 3b and c). These observations showed that TP-CDs might have a good ability to selectively bind RNA in cells, which could serve as an intracellular nucleolus-imaging nanoagent. It should be noted that with addition of RNA into N-free CDs aqueous solution, almost no fluorescence change was found (Fig. S5b), suggesting that N doping plays an important role in the affinity between RNA and TP-CDs. To reveal the nature of the interaction between TP-CDs and RNA, 1H NMR and zeta-potential measurements were carried out. As shown from the 1H NMR spectra of the TP-CD and RNA mixture (Fig. 3d), the chemical shifts in the range of 6.4–7.4 ppm were slightly downshifted by 0.1–0.2 ppm compared to those in the absence of RNA. Referring to previous studies on the interaction between dyes and RNA [66,67]. such 1H NMR change presumably resulted from the weak interaction between the N heterocyclic structure parts of TP-CDs and the bases of RNA. In addition, the zeta-potential measurement showed that TP-CDs have a positive charge of 34.9 ± 12.1 mV (Fig. S6), and the positive charge feature of TP-CDs is advantageous to the interaction with negative RNA. To prove this deduction, the charge variation of TP-CD surface was measured upon the addition of different concentrations of RNA. As shown in Fig. S7, as the concentration of RNA was gradually increased, zeta potential of TP-CD surface decreased linearly and finally remained unchanged around 0 mV, implying that there was an obvious electrostatic interaction between TP-CDs and RNA. In addition to 1H NMR and zeta-potential measurements, a theoretical simulation was also employed to investigate the cooperative interaction between TP-CDs and RNA via a simplified model using the quantum chemistry program package ORCA [68,69] and multifunctional wavefunction analyzer Multiwfn [70]. According to Fig. 4 a, the edge quinoline ring of TP-CDs displayed significant positive charge characteristics (blue color), while the graphitic core exhibited negative charge characteristics (red color). The positive charge characteristic of the quinoline ring is very favorable for interaction with negatively charged RNA bases, indicating that TP-CDs easily combine with RNA. To gain more in-depth insight into the origin of the combination of RNA and TP-CDs, the reduced density gradient (RDG, defined as Eq (1)) [71] was employed to analyze the interaction between TP-CDs and RNA in real space based on the electron density, where λ 2 represents the second eigenvalue of the electron density Hessian matrix.

The interaction regions can be visualized by RDG isosurfaces generated via visual molecular dynamics (VMD) [72]. The red regions indicate strong repulsion, the green and brown regions indicate van der Waals interactions, and the blue regions indicate strong, attractive interactions. As shown in Fig. 4b and c, it is evident that the N-heterocyclic ring of TP-CDs and RNA bases had attractive π−π interactions. Furthermore, quantitative analysis was carried out to analyze the role π−π interaction. Fig. 4d describes the plots of the RDG with the electron density multiplied by the sign of λ2. In the RNA and H2O system, observed spikes near 0.029 a.u. of [λ 2(r)]ρ(r)] (Fig. S8). However, in the RNA and TP-CDs systems, a spike region appeared in the range from −0.005 to −0.010 a.u. (Fig. 4d), indicating that the RNA bases' electrons transferred to the N heterocyclic ring of TP-CDs, and weak interactions were generated. Finally, to obtain the contribution of each part of the energy to the binding energy, we performed energy decomposition on the complex and analyzed their weak interactions. The data in Table S2 implied that electrostatic and π−π interactions contributed most of the binding energy. These results revealed that the π−π and electrostatic interactions between TP-CDs and RNA together induced affinity between TP-CDs and RNA.

After researching the interaction between TP-CDs and RNA, the application for cell imaging of TP-CDs was investigated by two-photon confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). HeLa cells were selected as the target cells. HeLa cells were incubated in a culture medium with TP-CDs for 3 h at a concentration of 50 μg mL−1. The two-photon CLSM images showed that HeLa cells were successfully labeled with strong red PL in the nucleolar region under excitation with a 700 nm two-photon laser (Fig. 5 ). Notably, a slight red PL was also found in the cytoplasm, but its intensity was lower than that in the nucleolus. Therefore, it could be inferred that TP-CDs could image intracellular nucleolus. Subsequently, we conducted colocalization experiments via the use of commercial colocalization dye RNA select. Since the absorption of TP-CDs and RNA overlaps, we used a two-photon laser to excite TP-CDs at 700 nm to avoid overlapping absorption. As seen in the two-photon CLSM image (Fig. 5a and b), both TP-CDs and RNA selection could stain the nucleolus. Furthermore, overlapping images showed that TP-CDs and selected RNA almost entirely stained the same site of the nucleolus (Fig. 5c). The analysis of the fluorescence intensity of the nucleolus staining site in the range of 1.5–4 μm revealed that the intensity change trend was consistent and Pearson's colocalization coefficients (Rr) around nucleolus was 0.91 (Fig. 5d and e). Since CDs are extremely variable in nature and their interactions are quite heterogeneous with respect to various cell lines, the imaging result with respect a particular cell line cannot be claimed as universality. Mouse breast cancer (4T1) cells were also selected as another kind of target cells. As shown in Fig. S9, similar imaging result was obtained from 4T1 cell line, suggesting that the imaging behavior of TP-CDs has universality. We used RNase and DNase, which can specifically hydrolyze RNA and DNA, to determine the RNA selectivity of TP-CDs in HeLa cells. Upon treatment of HeLa cells with RNase, a fluorescence decrease in the nucleolar region was observed by CLSM, whereas after treatment with DNase, a change in the nucleolar region was scarcely observed (Fig. S10 and S11). These phenomena clearly indicated that TP-CDs and RNA are effectively combined in the cell, confirming that TP-CDs are one type of outstanding two-photon fluorescence imaging nanoagent to stain the nucleolus. For understanding the pathway of TP-CDs, CLSM imaging of HeLa cells was performed under incubation with TP-CDs for 0.75 h in the different endocytosis inhibiting environments, including low temperature (4 °C), NaN3, colchicine (CLC), chlorpromazine (CPZ), and methyl-β-cyclodextrin (MβCD). As shown in Fig. S12, all of the HeLa cells coincubated with TP-CDs exhibited red fluorescence with a similar brightness in their nucleolus regions not only at different temperatures of 4 and 37 °C but also under treatment with various inhibitors, respectively. The quantitative analysis also indicated that the cell internalization of the TP-CDs was affected by neither different temperatures nor various inhibitors (Fig. S12), comfirming that TP-CDs have an extraordinary cell-penetrating ability in a temperature- and energy-independent fashion, similar to several already reported CDs [73,74].

ROS generation is vital to realize PDT. To estimate the ROS generation ability of TP-CDs under irradiation, we used DPBF to trap ROS. DPBF can directly oxidize by ROS (e.g., 1O2 and O2 •−), which induces a decrease in the absorption spectra [75]. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the absorption centered at 418 nm decreased by 60% under 638 nm laser irradiation (1.0 W cm−1) for 5 min with TP-CDs. As a control, absorption spectra were also obtained for nonirradiated DPBF in the presence of TP-CDs and irradiated DPBF in the absence of TP-CDs. Insignificant absorption changes were observed for the control groups (Fig. S13). These results confirmed that 638 nm laser irradiation of TP-CDs is essential to generate ROS.

Next, to reveal ROS generation, an electron spin resonance (ESR) experiment was performed with two spin trap agents: TEMP and DMPO. After irradiation with a 638 nm laser, the signal of the adduct of TEMP with 1O2 (TEMPO) was found, revealing 1O2 generation under irradiation (Fig. 6b, black line). No ESR signal of TEMPO was observed in the presence of TP-CDs without laser irradiation (Fig. 6b, blue line) or under 638 nm laser irradiation without TP-CDs (Fig. 6b, red line). •OH and O2 •− production was also studied by ESR via the use of DMPO after 638 nm laser irradiation of TP-CDs. The signals of adducts of DMPO with •OH and O2 •− were not found (Fig. S14, black line). These phenomena revealed that 638 nm laser irradiation of TP-CDs could cause 1O2 production or •OH or O2 •− production.

The key to implementing PDT is intracellular ROS production. Here, we used DCFH-DA to assume the intracellular production of 1O2 photoinduced by TP-CDs in HeLa cells. As shown in Fig. 6c, the fluorescence of the oxide product dichlorofluorescein (DCF) was observed with TP-CDs under 638 nm laser irradiation. In contrast, no noticeable fluorescence was observed in cells without TP-CDs in the presence of laser irradiation (control group) or with TP-CDs without laser irradiation. This observation indicated that intracellular ROS could powerfully be generated with TP-CDs under 638 nm laser irradiation, which has enormous potential to further reach efficient PDT.

Encouraged by the abovementioned results, we used an assay based on MTT to estimate the in vitro cell-killing efficacy of TP-CDs. As shown in Fig. 7 a, when the concentration of TP-CDs ranged from 0 to 100 μg mL−1, the cell viability of TP-CDs without laser irradiation was greater than 90%, demonstrating that TP-CDs without no irradiation have negligible cytotoxicity. In contrast, when irradiated by a 638 nm laser (1 W cm−2) for 5 min, the cell viability was only 22% at a TP-CD concentration of 100 μg mL−1. To further assess the photodynamic cell-killing efficacy of TP-CDs, a live/dead assay was carried out using HeLa cells. PI and calcein-AM were employed to differentiate dead cells (red) from living cells (green). As seen from Fig. 7b, cells in the nonirradiated group after incubation with TP-CDs were stained green fluorescence by calcein-AM, and no dead cells stained red fluorescence by PI were observed, proving the intrinsically negligible cytotoxicity of TP-CDs in the absence of irradiation. After 638 nm laser irradiation (1.0 W cm−2) for 5 min, bright red fluorescence of PI was detected. Merging the fluorescence images before and after irradiation showed that the red fluorescent signal of PI arises from the intracellular region. As a control, laser irradiation of cells was also performed in the absence of TP-CDs, inducing almost no cell death. These observations further confirmed that TP-CDs under 638 nm laser irradiation could effectively cause cell death, which agrees well with the MTT results. In addition, when considering that the precursor of PAB is highly toxic and good biocompatibility of TP-CDs is crucial to further biomedical applications, in vivo biocompatibility of TP-CDs was studied using histological examination of the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney. Female Balb/c mice were chosen for in vivo tests via intravenous administration of TP-CDs. All of the examined organs showed no obvious acute, chronic pathological toxicity and adverse effects after 14 days (Fig. S15), further proving that TP-CDs have benign biocompatibility. The results indicated that the occurrence of decomposition, intermolecular cyclization, and condensation reactions during the formation of CDs could make the biocompatibility of TP-CDs different from PAB and greatly improve their biocompatibility, similar to other already reported CDs [76,77].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro real-time dynamic monitoring of nucleolar changes induced by PDT
ce:section.ce:para: ROS have an ultrashort lifetime (less than 40 ns) and a tiny diffusion distance (≈20 nm) [78,79]. As a result, the distance between PSs and biomacromolecules has a substantial impact on ROS's bioactivity. For TP-CDs, specific complexation between TP-CDs and RNA may significantly affect PDT efficacy via RNA damage. The impact of the complexation between TP-CDs and RNA in the nucleolus on PDT efficacy in HeLa cells was further explored by MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 8 a, upon 638 nm laser irradiation for 5 min (1.0 W cm−1), the cell viability was 45% for cells incubated with only TP-CDs and 47% for cells incubated with TP-CDs pretreated with DNA, whereas it was 75% for cells incubated with TP-CDs pretreated with RNA. The cell viability of the TP-CD + RNA group was higher than those of only the TP-CD and TP-CD + DNA groups, suggesting that specific complexation between TP-CDs and RNA is important for the enhancement of PDT. To further investigate the possible mechanism of enhanced PDT via the complexation between TP-CDs and RNA, fluorescence spectra and gel electrophoresis were performed. As shown in Fig. 7d and S16, the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution gradually decreased with increasing irradiation time, which is consistent with the result of Fig. 3c, indicating that TP-CDs under irradiation could lead to the degradation of RNA. Another piece of evidence to prove this conclusion is the result obtained from gel electrophoresis. As shown in Fig. 7e, the RNA in the absence of TP-CDs under irradiation showed three typical bands of an intact RNA structure, assigned to 28S rRNA, 18S rRNA, and 5S rRNA. However, no clear bands were observed for the RNA in the presence of TP-CDs irradiated by 638 nm laser irradiation for 5 min, similar to the result of RNA in the presence of RNAse. These results illustrated that ROS production photoinduced by TP-CDs could effectively degrade RNA. Particularly when considering that ROS have a short diffusion distance and specific complexation between TP-CDs and RNA can shorten the distance between each other, the PDT efficacy induced by such photodegradation of RNA can be enhanced.

Treatment in combination with real-time monitoring of intracellular dynamic changes is of great significance to evaluate therapeutic efficacy [80,81]. Unlike other therapeutic nanoplatforms that are usually insusceptible to surrounding environmental changes caused by treatment, our TP-CDs can image the nucleolus because selective interaction between RNA and TP-CDs can cause an increase in its fluorescence intensity. Meanwhile, the ROS photoinduced by TP-CDs can degrade RNA into fragments, which may release TP-CDs from the TP-CDs@RNA complex and reduce the TP-CDs' fluorescence intensity, providing huge possibilities for real-time fluorescence monitoring of dynamic nucleolus changes during the therapeutic process. In vitro real-time fluorescence monitoring of dynamic nucleolar changes was performed in the treatment process by CLSM using HeLa cells. The nucleolar region of HeLa cells displayed strong red fluorescence before 638 nm laser irradiation (Fig. 8a), whereas red fluorescence in the nucleolar region became weak after irradiation for 1 min (Fig. 8b) and 3 min (Fig. 8c). After further irradiation, fluorescence in the nucleolar region completely disappeared due to the degradation of RNA by photoinduced ROS (Fig. 8d). This result was identical to the MTT result, indicating that TP-CDs can effectively realize real-time fluorescence monitoring of dynamic nucleolar changes during the therapeutic process. It is beneficial to evaluate the therapeutic efficacy maximizing therapeutic efficacy, and reducing side effects. In addition, the two-photon excitation and red emission characteristics of TP-CDs can enable real-time fluorescence monitoring to avoid interference from background absorption and emission effectively. However, it should be noted that despite TP-CDs can be successfully applied for treatment in combination of real-time monitoring of nucleolus in vitro, it is still hard to achieve in vivo therapeutic monitoring of nucleolus via use of current TP-CDs, when considering that it is a very complex process that the access of TP-CDs to the RNA of the nucleolus in living tissues. Therefore, to achieve in vivo therapeutic monitoring application, we will need to consider more factors on the design of CDs in the future work.

In summary, we prepared a new class of TP-CDs via N doping strategy with the following attractive characteristics: red fluorescence, two-photon excitation, photodynamic activity, self-targetability of nucleolus via specific affinity with RNA, and fluorescence variation during the therapeutic process. Among these characteristics, the latter three characteristics can enable TP-CDs to effectively realize real-time fluorescence monitoring of dynamic nucleolar changes during the therapeutic process, offering further therapeutic efficacy evaluation. It is highly beneficial to maximize therapeutic efficacy and reduce side effects. In addition, the two-photon and red emitting characteristics of TP-CDs can enable real-time fluorescence monitoring to validly avoid interference from background absorption and emission. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example of red emissive and two-photon CDs to be applied for PDT in combination with real-time dynamic monitoring of dynamic organelle changes. It is believed that the present research not only paves the way to use “smart” CDs for the realization of organelle-targeted treatment in combination with real-time dynamic changes but also deepens the understanding of the effect of therapeutic behaviors on intracellular dynamic changes.
